

**Predicting Shelter Adoption Rates: Decision Tree & k-Nearest Neighbor**

Amber Grace Hulsey  
DA 460: Predictive Data Analytics  
Dr. Jason Imbrogno   
November 13, 2024

**Predicting Shelter Adoption Rates**  
---

Predicting shelter adoption rates would help shelters know how effective they are in finding homes for the animals they house. Comparing states would also help identify areas for improvement. An animal shelter is a temporary housing unit for animals that do not have a home or were lost, abandoned, or given up. This topic is relevant because numerous animals are being thrown into shelters. According to the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals, “Approximately 7.6 million companion animals enter animal shelters nationwide every year” (*Community Care College*, 2023). To add onto this statistic, about “2.7 million shelter animals are adopted each year” (*Community Care College*, 2023). This leaves around 4.9 million animals in shelters to never be adopted. The issue is that more animals are being put into shelters than being adopted. Because these shelters are supposed to be temporary, this begs the question of how long an animal can stay in a shelter. Some states only require a minimum of 48-72 hours of a holding period before the shelter can decide if the animal will be adopted or euthanized. Roughly 30 states have a minimum holding period of five to seven days, which is still a short amount of time. The longest holding period requirement is 8 days before the animal can be euthanized in the state of Maine (*State Holding Period Laws for Impounded Animals | Animal Legal & Historical Center*, 2017). Whether a state has a longer average holding period depends on how many animals come into the shelters. Predicting a state’s adoption rate would benefit the state because it helps draw attention to which states have the highest desire to adopt an animal and which states lack it.   
I am using five independent variables that I believe best predict shelter adoption rates. All of the variables that I have chosen are numeric data. My dependent variable, the shelter adoption rate, is also numeric. I used [https://www.creelighting.com/insights/article/](https://www.creelighting.com/insights/article/the-states-that-adopt-from-animal-shelters-the-most/) to record the adoption rates by state. Using the adoption rate and not the number of adoptions is more beneficial because the rate is per 1000 people. This eliminates the bias shown for states with a higher population. The prediction model is measured by state, so there are 50 observations. Washington, D.C., is not included in this data set since it is not considered a state but a federal district. The year that is utilized for all variables is 2022\. This is exceptionally recent data and one of the highest years for more animals in shelters. One main reason for the more significant numbers is the effects of COVID-19, which affected all consumption by people, including pets. The independent variables I chose are median household income, education rate, median age, average household size, and women’s marriage rate, again, all measured at the state level. Median household income is one of the most significant to measure because pets can be expensive. According to *PetExec,* “The average pet owner spends nearly $1,400 a year on each pet,” but the author notes that this number can be higher if the pet has more needs or the owner has more than one pet (PetExec, 2024). The higher one’s income is, the more ‘extra’ money they have for a pet. The source [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?rid=249&eid=259515&od=2022-01-01#) is where the median household income comes from.   
A state’s education rate should be included because someone with more education might have less time for a pet due to their career. On the other hand, someone who is more educated might have more money to spend on a pet. The education rate, found in the source [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?rid=330&eid=391444&od=2022-01-01#), is arguably one of the most important variables to consider. This is because one’s career highly impacts how much free time one has for leisure, kids, and pets. Median age is vital because someone older might want a pet to avoid loneliness; however, they could have a more challenging time caring for a pet. The data can be found here: [https://www.statista.com/statistics/](https://www.statista.com/statistics/208048/median-age-of-population-in-the-usa-by-state/College). College students more than likely do not have the time or money for a pet; however, their parents might. Millennials, currently between the ages of 28 and 43, are the generation with the highest percentage of pet ownership (Megna, 2023).   
A state’s average household size, which can be found in the source [https://data.census.gov/map](https://data.census.gov/map?tid=ACSDT1Y2022.B25010&layer=VT_2022_040_00_PP_D1&loc=38.4047,-104.8294,z3.4738), needs to be measured because someone with many children might not consider having a pet because their time and money are being spent elsewhere. Many people who do not have any kids tend to have pets instead. However, some parents might get a pet solely for the entertainment and morale of their children. One would expect this variable to potentially be highly correlated with the dependent variable since the relationship between the two theoretically moves together, increasing or decreasing. As shown later in *Table 1*, however, there is a low correlation between the two. Lastly, the women's marriage rate is essential because a married woman has another person's income working together and could better financially support a pet. This information can be found here: [https://www.bgsu.edu/ncfmr/resources/data/](https://www.bgsu.edu/ncfmr/resources/data/family-profiles/loo-marriage-rate-US-geographic-variation-2022-fp-23-23.html). Alternatively, if someone is unmarried, they could want an animal to keep them company. I chose to use the women’s marriage rate instead of the total marriage rate because it is statistically said to be more reliable when discussing family formation and planning (Fry, 2010). Since adopting a pet is part of family formation, focusing on the women’s marriage rate makes more sense.   
*Section 1: The following section contains a summary of each variable, data source, and the measurement.*   
**Dependent Variable:**

- Shelter Adoption Rate: Measured as the number of people in a state who adopt an animal from a shelter per 1,000 people. Data Source: CREE Lighting. [https://www.creelighting.com/insights/article/the-states-that-adopt-from-animal-shelters-the-most/](https://www.creelighting.com/insights/article/the-states-that-adopt-from-animal-shelters-the-most/), State level data for 2022\.

**Independent Variables:**

- Median Household Income: The income level a household earns in each state, where half the households earn more and half earn less. Data Source: FRED. [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?rid=249\&eid=259515\&od=2022-01-01\#,](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?rid=249&eid=259515&od=2022-01-01#) State-level data for 2022\.  
- Median Age: The age in a state where half of the population is younger and half is older. Data Source: Statistica. [https://www.statista.com/statistics/208048/median-age-of-population-in-the-usa-by-state/](https://www.statista.com/statistics/208048/median-age-of-population-in-the-usa-by-state/), State-level data for 2022\.  
- Average Household Size: The number of people living in an occupied housing unit divided by the total number of occupied housing units. Data Source: US Census. [https://data.census.gov/map?tid=ACSDT1Y2022.B25010\&layer=VT\_2022\_040\_00\_PP\_D1\&loc=38.4047,-104.8294,z3.4738](https://data.census.gov/map?tid=ACSDT1Y2022.B25010&layer=VT_2022_040_00_PP_D1&loc=38.4047,-104.8294,z3.4738), State-level data for 2022\.  
- Women’s Marriage Rate: The total number of women in a state who are married per 1000 unmarried women population aged 15 and older. Data Source: Bowling Green State University. [https://www.bgsu.edu/ncfmr/resources/data/family-profiles/loo-marriage-rate-US-geographic-variation-2022-fp-23-23.html](https://www.bgsu.edu/ncfmr/resources/data/family-profiles/loo-marriage-rate-US-geographic-variation-2022-fp-23-23.html), State-level data for 2022\.  
- Education Rate: Measured as the percentage of individuals in a state who have a bachelor's degree or higher. Data Source: FRED. [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?rid=330\&eid=391444\&od=2022-01-01\#](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?rid=330&eid=391444&od=2022-01-01#) State-level data for 2022\.

*Table 1: Correlation Matrix*

| Variable: | Adoption Rate | Median Income | Median Age | Average Household Size | Women’s Marriage Rate | Education Rate |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Adoption Rate | 1.00 |  |  |  |  |  |
| Median Income | \-0.29 | 1.00 |  |  |  |  |
| Median Age | 0.06 | 0.44 | 1.00 |  |  |  |
| Average Household Size | \-0.18 | 0.31 | 0.12 | 1.00 |  |  |
| Women’s Marriage Rate | \-0.19 | 0.17 | \-0.09 | 0.17 | 1.00 |  |
| Education Rate | \-0.34 | 0.57 | 0.18 | 0.07 | 0.27 | 1.00 |

As seen in *Table 1*, the shelter adoption rate is negatively correlated with median income, women’s marriage rate, and education rate. This means that, on average, a state with a lower income is more likely to adopt a pet from the shelter. Also, on average, the lower the state’s women’s marriage rate, the more likely the state is to adopt from the shelter. According to the last correlation, the lower a state’s education rate, the higher its pet adoption rate. All of these outcomes are justifiable. Having a lower income can mean their job allows for more time spent with an animal. Not being married encourages a companion for company. The education rate and the median income have similar justifications, which makes sense because their correlation is positively correlated. Also seen in *Table 1*, median age and median income are positively correlated, which indicates that states with an older median age may have higher median incomes. This is verifiable because the older someone is, the more annual income they can grow through their experience.   
	One independent variable that I did not include is allergy rates, but could also be used to determine a state’s shelter adoption rate. According to the Asthma and Allergy Foundation in America, about 1 in 4 adults in the United States experience some kind of allergy (*Allergy Facts*, 2024). People allergic to cats/dogs are less likely to have a pet due to reactions and sickness. I did not use allergies because the data was complex to find on a state level, and there are many different types of allergies. Also, I would need to conduct survey data, as some people are unsure if they are allergic to a specific animal or multiple. Since my other five variables are more accurate representations, I chose not to include allergies. Another independent variable that could be utilized in this model is the region that the state is in. These regions include west, northwest, southwest, mid-west, southeast, mid-Atlantic, and northeast. This variable would be interesting to include to see if the area that the state is in impacts the shelter adoption rate. While these two variables could be helpful, I did not include them in my model.  
*Table 2: Summary Statistics*

| Variable: | Min | Max | Mean | St. Dev. |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Shelter Adoption Rate (Dependent) | 1.32 | 12.34 | 5.58 | 2.49 |
| Median Household Income | 50540 | 112500 | 77909.40 | 13171.26 |
| Median Age | 32.10 | 45.10 | 39.33 | 2.15 |
| Average Household Size | 2.23 | 2.95 | 2.46 | 0.14 |
| Women’s Marriage Rate | 20.80 | 42.80 | 32.40 | 4.91 |
| Education Rate | 24.80 | 46.60 | 34.64 | 5.49 |

	As seen in *Table 2*, the shelter adoption rate has a relatively wide range for the values. For the maximum value, which comes from New Mexico, we can translate it as per 1000 people, 12.34 people adopt a pet from the shelter. The main reason for this high statistic, without considering the variables in this prediction model, is because of the Animal Humane group located in this state that pushes for pet adoptions. On the other hand, the minimum value of 1.32 coming from Connecticut, translates as per 1000 people, only 1.32 people adopt a pet from the shelter. According to *CT Insider,* one common reason for Connecticut’s lower rate is because of their financial struggles after COVID-19 (Danseyar, 2023). Another wide range between the minimum and maximum values is the education rate. The minimum value is from both Mississippi and West Virginia while the maximum value is from Massachusetts. According to *World Population Review,* Massachusetts is known as one of the smartest states due to its high-quality education system. Southern states tend to have lower-quality education systems (*Smartest States 2024*, 2024). An interesting point about the women’s marriage rate is that the minimum value of 20.80 comes from New Mexico, which also had the highest shelter adoption rate. This means New Mexico has the fewest married women but the most pets adopted from shelters. It is essential to note this relationship because it could be related. As mentioned, unmarried individuals may adopt a pet to improve their morale and reduce loneliness.   
 	For this specific research project, I will use two prediction models: a decision tree and a k-nearest neighbor. This data set has no missing values, so I did not have to impute any values. I did need to do minimum-maximum normalization on the input variable income since its values are not initially comparable to the others. This data transformation allowed for a more accurate outcome and interpretation. When using a decision tree, each node represents a discrete test on an input attribute value, and the final leaf represents predictions of outcome variables. This model type is structured like a flow chart. I utilized Python 3.12 to build the decision tree and the k-nearest neighbor model to predict the shelter adoption rate utilizing the five independent variables. For the k-NN, I first used Python to choose the best “k” value for the prediction model. The “k” being used is three because it performed the best in Python within the Cross Validation Test. When running each model, the Python software splits the data into the training and test data sets. This is to check the outcomes and perform an analysis to determine which model is best. The Euclidean Distance metric is used for this model since it handles numeric data extremely well. I will choose the model with the lowest sum of squared errors (SSE) to make this decision. The lowest SSE is desired because this means the error term is the smallest. The smaller the SSE, the more accurate the model is. The training set is 35 observations and the test set is 15 observations. I utilized the entire test set in each model but will only show the first five below for your reference.   
*Table 3: Test Set Observations and Outcome for Decision Tree and k-Nearest Neighbor*

| Median Income | Median Age | Average Household Size | Women’s Marriage Rate | Education Rate | Actual Adoption Rate | Predicted Adoption Rate (Decision Tree) | Predicted Adoption Rate (k-NN) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 77600 | 35.6 | 2.65 | 36.8 | 33.9 | 4.52 | 5.23 | 8.57 |
| 79350 | 38.9 | 2.33 | 33.4 | 32.3 | 5.09 | 6.19 | 5.47 |
| 112500 | 39.7 | 2.54 | 28.7 | 43.8 | 3.13 | 3.12 | 2.78 |
| 74360 | 39.1 | 2.38 | 35.7 | 32.2 | 5.18 | 7.88 | 7.90 |
| 75870 | 40.2 | 2.36 | 35.4 | 34.6 | 8.51 | 2.77 | 7.12 |

*Table 4: Decision Tree*  
*![][image1]*  
	*Table 4* provides a picture of the decision tree. According to the first split, the input variable education rate is seen as most important in predicting the pet adoption rate. There are many reasons for this, but the most important is that the more education someone has, generally the more income they have. This would allow for more money to be spent on adopting a pet. I limited the depth to 20 for the decision tree so the model would avoid overfitting and have a higher accuracy.   
*Table 5: Total SSE*

| Model: | Decision Tree | k-Nearest Neighbor |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **SSE:** | 45.12 | 45.32 |

	  
After computing the SSE for the decision tree and k-nearest neighbor, I compared the values to choose the best model. As seen in *Table 5*, the decision tree’s SSE is 45.12, while the k-NN is 45.32. Because SSE is measuring the error, the lowest value is preferred. In this instance, the decision tree has a slightly lower SSE, so this is the better model of the two. There are a few reasons why the decision tree outperformed the k-NN for this project. Decision trees provide a clear, rule-based learning structure that helps easily explain shelter adoption rates. Also, decision trees handle non-linear relationships better than k-NN. Even though there were no extreme outliers, there were a few odd inputs, such as the median income for Maryland being $112,500. Decision trees handle outliers better than k-NN because the splits use the mean or median of the values, which diminishes the effect the outliers may have.   
	Overall, both models performed well. The most surprising component is how close the sum of squared errors were to one another. Choosing the decision tree based on the SSE was difficult, considering the difference was only 0.20. To improve this model, I could have a panel data set, which would include the United States over multiple years instead of just one. More data would be highly beneficial, enabling more precise splits in the decision tree and improving accuracy in the k-nearest neighbors algorithm. There are a couple of different ways I could further my research. It would be intriguing to see if geographics influence pet adoption rates. Based on my data, the western states, on average, have a higher pet adoption rate. Also, it might be interesting to see if seasonality plays a role. For example, many people adopt pets as presents for Christmas. There are many different studies that could be done in the future by utilizing both models’ results. 

References

*‌Allergy Facts. (2024, April 19). Asthma & Allergy Foundation of America. https://aafa.org/allergies/allergy-facts/\#:\~:text=More%20than%20100%20million%20people,%2C%20eczema%2C%20and%20food%20allergies.\&text=Nearly%201%20in%203%20U.S.,%2C%20eczema%2C%20or%20food%20allergy.*

*Community Care College. (2023, April 3). Community Care College. https://communitycarecollege.edu/veterinary-assistant/pet-facts/*

*‌Danseyar, S. (2023, July 28). CT animal shelters filling up as fewer people are adopting pets. CT Insider. https://www.ctinsider.com/journalinquirer/article/ct-animal-shelters-pet-adoptions-18253769.php*

*FRED. (2024). Stlouisfed.org; Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/*

*Fry, R. (2010, January 19). Women, Men and the New Economics of Marriage. Pew Research Center. https://www.pewresearch.org/social-trends/2010/01/19/women-men-and-the-new-economics-of-marriage/*  
*Home \- Cree Lighting. (2022). Creelighting.com. https://www.creelighting.com/*  
*Marriage Rate in the U.S.: Geographic Variation, 2022*. (2022). Bowling Green State University. https://www.bgsu.edu/ncfmr/resources/data/family-profiles/loo-marriage-rate-US-geographic-variation-2022-fp-23-23.html

*‌Megna, M. (2023, April 10). Pet Ownership Statistics 2024\. Forbes Advisor. https://www.forbes.com/advisor/pet-insurance/pet-ownership-statistics/*

*‌PetExec. (2024). Pet Industry Trends: Pet Spending Statistics in 2024\. Petexec.net; PetExec. https://www.petexec.net/resources/marketing/pet-spending-statistics\#:\~:text=The%20average%20pet%20owner%20spends,spend%20money%20on%20each%20year.*

*Smartest States 2024\. (2024). Worldpopulationreview.com. https://worldpopulationreview.com/state-rankings/smartest-states*

*‌State Holding Period Laws for Impounded Animals | Animal Legal & Historical Center. (2017). Animallaw.info. https://www.animallaw.info/topic/state-holding-period-laws-impounded-animals\#:\~:text=Over%20thirty%20states%20have%20what,from%20five%20to%20seven%20days.*

*Statista \- The Statistics Portal. (2024). Statista; Statista. https://www.statista.com/*
